pattern: Grouping node values by their depth turns the tree into a plain list of level totals, so the whole problem reduces to sorting that short list and picking the kth entry from the top.
hints: Every node's contribution only depends on which depth it sits at, so process the tree one full level at a time and keep a running total per level.
Once you have the list of level sums, the answer is just about ranking them — sort the list from largest to smallest.
If k is larger than how many levels the tree actually has, there is no kth largest sum to report, so return -1 in that case; otherwise return the value at position k-1 in the sorted list.

edge_inputs.description: smallest possible tree, root plus one child, asking for the largest level
smallest possible tree, asking for the second and last level
k exceeds the number of levels actually present
left-skewed chain, sums strictly decrease by level
two different levels happen to sum to the same value
asymmetric tree with several levels, asking for a middle rank
asking for the smallest level sum by requesting the last rank

stress.description: random near-complete tree serialized level-order, random k within its level count
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(2, max(2, size))
    vals = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    import math
    levels = int(math.log2(n)) + 1
    k = rng.randint(1, levels)
    return [vals, k]
